text.title: Validation Process and Agent Limitations
text.summary: Five-step validation process, kill-threshold discipline, and agent limitations for problem validation.

rule.statement: Run validation in five steps: (1) state the problem hypothesis; (2) define the kill threshold before collecting any evidence; (3) collect evidence from 10+ data points across multiple source types; (4) label each piece of evidence with its hierarchy tier; (5) decide PROCEED, PIVOT (with direction), or KILL.
rule.rationale: Defining the kill threshold first prevents post-hoc rationalization of weak evidence. The hierarchy labeling step forces the researcher to confront whether evidence is behavioral or just stated.

example.note: Kill threshold defined upfront: "If fewer than 3 out of 10 interviewees confirm the pain at intensity 7+/10, we stop." After 10 interviews: 7/10 confirmed, avg intensity 8.2. Decision: PROCEED to solution validation.

rule.statement: Distinguish problem frequency (how often it occurs) from problem intensity (how much it hurts); both must exceed the threshold for a build decision.
rule.rationale: A daily-but-minor problem and a rare-but-catastrophic problem require different solutions; conflating them produces a product that addresses neither adequately.

rule.statement: A competitor existing is weak evidence (someone thinks the problem is real); a competitor churning customers is strong evidence (the problem persists unsolved).
rule.rationale: Markets with active competitors validate demand but not opportunity; churn data from those competitors validates that the problem remains unsolved at scale.

rule.statement: LLMs cannot interview real users, cannot assess commitment signals from text alone, and will exhibit availability bias toward problems common in training data; use agents only to design the validation protocol and synthesize collected evidence.
rule.rationale: LLMs trained on public text overrepresent developer pain and underrepresent niche B2B pain; agent-generated PROCEED recommendations based on stated interest must be treated as PIVOT until behavioral evidence is collected.

text.reference: LLM prompt to score evidence and recommend decision
